comments.author: @Catastrophickakistrocracy
comments.text: I retired six years ago and then spent four years researching Caribbean, Central and South American countries to relocate to for my “golden years”- at least for the winters.  I visited fifteen countries/islands and decided on Nicaragua.  It was an easy decision.  Best weather, best home prices, incredibly safe, great food, tons of activities, breathtaking and nearly empty beaches, tons of personal freedoms, excellent and cheap healthcare and most importantly, the most humble, kind, fun-loving, sincere people I’ve ever met.  They love God, value family and are very welcoming to newcomers.

comments.author: @playamariananicaragua
comments.text:
Excellent video. Nicaragua is one of the most misunderstood countries in the world, yet the people who actually spend time there quickly recognize something very different from the headlines.

The beauty is real. The coastline is massive. The people are warm. The cost of living still makes sense. And the infrastructure quietly continues to improve year after year.

What many overlook is that Nicaragua still feels early. Not overbuilt. Not saturated. Still authentic.

I’ve spent over 20 years building and investing there and one thing becomes clear very quickly: the narrative from outside the country and the experience on the ground are often two completely different realities.

The Pacific coast especially is beginning to transform in ways most people still do not fully see yet.

Great job capturing a side of Nicaragua the world needs to see more often.

replies.author: @BestTravel2
replies.text: Thanks for sharing your extensive experience and insights! It's truly valuable to hear from someone who's spent so much time there, and I completely agree that the on-the-ground reality often differs from the headlines.

comments.author: @williamessig2610
comments.text: Lived in Granada Nicaragua for 2 years 2015 - 2018, and want to go back. Granada is the Oldest City Continually lived in All of the Americas. Established 1524. The Bus Systems in Central America are great. I could get on a modern bus and travel to Costa Rica and Panama which I did. One particular time,  a young Woman sat next to me on the bus. We said Hola and then when we left the station,  she leaned over, her head on my shoulder and slept for an hour when she reached her Community, nothing Romantic just a thing. Many people brought sleeping bags that they would get into head to toe because of the A C.  My Spam was basically poor but if i had a question many of the people that spoke English would answer any questions i had. Sometimes just tips on what will happen at the Border Crossings.  Peaceful Country although about 6 years ago there was an uprising as President was going to abolish Pensions. Lasted about a year then back to normal.

replies.author: @Vvrroommm
replies.text:
Not good for black people.. even the folks from Corn Island are similar to foreigners (outsiders) coming to the mainland.. some from Corn Island only speak English.. 
I have connected with some cool Nicaraguans  but mostly they stick to their own kind and outsiders are not welcomed with openness and kindness. 
Whiter you are the better human you are is the view in the world in general. This is a narrative that is programmed in most countries. 
Even a Mexican female that was in Nicaragua same time I was there felt the disconnect and the language was not exactly the same.. 98% of the Nicaraguans were not welcoming in places you would expect good customer service. 
No generic greeting . Just stares or half.hearted limp like a fish handshake energy. 
Mostly the ladies were rude.. And the ladies that were not rude were a delite and a pleasant encounter.. one lady even cooked an entire bag of arroz and frijoles, I purchased from the grocery store, for me at no cost. 
Another lady exchanged whatsapp numbers to help me with connecting with some business connects.. and happy to help. 
I met one old older gentlemen became good pals/buddies who share intimate info with me of his sons, father and experiences in politics that would being him to tears.. I felt honored to be the one there to experience those precious moments with him.. then we would get some fanta or coke cola, pan, arroz n frijoles or I would get galliopinto, queso, pico digalo, chile, limon, plaintain/banana fritas, cafe cream sugar

comments.text: A few years ago went on vacation there..loved it very much...cheap and people very nice BUT Ortega turned me against it all !!!!!! And I so very much wanted to live there.....

replies.author: @Mr.DamianM
replies.text: @JRGUSC I can only speak for myself. I went down there every year from 2013 to 2023. The first 8 years were great. But then the mood changed. Personally, I got fed up with getting pulled over on my motorcycle almost every time I went out. Airport customs helped themselves to my possessions on the way in and the way out. The people, even my long time friends, began to get an edge to them, less tolerant, less friendly. Our homeowner  association became downright militant. The final straw was a cop bribing me out of $30 to get my drivers license back. I felt targeted. When the prez is constantly spewing anti-capitalism and anti-Americanism, the people eventually believe it. It wasn't anything drastic, or any single event, just a slow realization that I wasn't wanted there anymore.

replies.author: @JRGUSC
replies.text: ​@Mr.DamianMCan both of you elaborate a little more on this sentiment?  Other than the obvious events that happened in 2018.

replies.author: @Mr.DamianM
replies.text: Same here. Sold the house there, no plans to go back until something changes.
